bytes.topic: Routing — defining routes, route parameters, grouping and prefixing
bytes.title: Fastify Routing — Parameters, Prefixes, and Plugin-Based Grouping
bytes.body:
Fastify routes are registered via `fastify.get()`, `fastify.post()`, or the generic `fastify.route()` with a full config object. Route parameters use `:param` syntax and land in `request.params` — wildcard segments use `*` for catch-all matching.

Grouping routes under a shared prefix is done through `fastify.register()` with the `prefix` option. Each registered plugin gets its own encapsulated scope, so hooks, decorators, and error handlers defined inside don't leak out. This is Fastify's core encapsulation model — not just a naming convenience.

For typed parameters, pair your route with a JSON Schema on `schema.params`. Fastify validates and coerces inputs before your handler runs, so `request.params.id` is already the right type — no manual parsing needed.

Use `fastify.register()` to split route groups into separate files, passing the `fastify` instance into each plugin. This keeps route modules fully self-contained and composable without a global router object.

bytes.topic: ORM & Database — query builders vs full ORMs, N+1 prevention, migrations
bytes.title: fastify + Drizzle ORM — Killing N+1 Without Losing Type Safety
bytes.body:
Fastify pairs naturally with lightweight query builders like Drizzle or Kysely over heavyweight ORMs like Sequelize. Heavy ORMs obscure SQL, make N+1 queries trivial to introduce, and bloat cold-start time — a real problem when Fastify's speed is your selling point.

With Drizzle, register a single db instance via `fastify.decorate` so every route shares one connection pool. For N+1, never fetch relations in a loop. Instead, use Drizzle's `with` clause (relational queries) or a manual JOIN — one query, full type inference. Drizzle's `db.query.posts.findMany({ with: { author: true } })` compiles to a single SQL JOIN, not N round trips.

For migrations, run `drizzle-kit push` (dev) or `drizzle-kit generate` + `migrate` (prod) before your Fastify server starts. Wire this into your `onReady` hook or a pre-start script in your Dockerfile — never inside a route handler. Schema drift between your TypeScript types and actual DB columns becomes a compile-time error, not a 3am incident.

bytes.topic: Dependency Injection — built-in DI containers vs manual wiring, testability impact
bytes.title: Fastify's Plugin System IS Your DI Container
bytes.body:
Fastify doesn't ship a traditional DI container — it ships something better: a plugin-scoped encapsulation model via `fastify-plugin` and `decorate`. Every dependency lives on the `fastify` instance, injected via `fastify.decorate()` and scoped by the plugin tree. Child plugins inherit parent decorations; siblings are isolated unless you use `fastify-plugin` to break encapsulation upward.

This matters for testability. Instead of mocking a global service registry, you build a test instance and register only the plugins your handler needs. Swap a real `pg` pool for a `pgmock` by registering a different plugin before your route plugin — the handler never changes.

Manual wiring (passing deps through closures or module singletons) breaks this model. You lose scoping, you can't swap implementations per test, and you fight Fastify's lifecycle instead of using it. The `fastify.decorate` + `fastify-plugin` pattern is the idiomatic DI — use it as the framework intends, and testability is nearly free.

bytes.topic: Project Structure — MVC vs vertical slice, feature folders, layering conventions
bytes.title: Fastify Project Structure — Vertical Slices Beat MVC at Scale
bytes.body:
Fastify's plugin system is purpose-built for vertical slice architecture. Each feature registers itself as an encapsulated plugin via `fastify.register()`, scoping its routes, decorators, hooks, and schemas internally — MVC layers fight this by spreading a single feature across `controllers/`, `services/`, and `models/` directories, making encapsulation impossible.

A vertical slice in Fastify means one folder per feature: `src/features/users/` contains `routes.ts`, `schema.ts`, `service.ts`, and `repository.ts`. The routes file calls `fastify.register()` on the feature plugin, which in turn registers child plugins for sub-resources. This maps directly onto Fastify's directed acyclic plugin graph — decorators added inside a plugin don't leak to siblings, so `users/` can decorate `fastify.userRepo` without polluting `orders/`.

Use `@fastify/autoload` to mount feature folders automatically. Point it at `src/features/` and it traverses subdirectories, calling `register()` on every `index.ts` it finds. Layering convention: routes validate with JSON Schema (Fastify compiles these via fast-json-stringify), delegate to a service, which owns business logic, and call a repository that wraps raw `pg` or Prisma queries. No framework coupling below the route layer.
bytes.code_snippet:
// src/features/users/index.ts
import { FastifyPluginAsync } from 'fastify'
import { getUserSchema, createUserSchema } from './schema'
import { UserService } from './service'

const usersPlugin: FastifyPluginAsync = async (fastify) => {
  const service = new UserService(fastify.pg) // pg decorated at app root

  fastify.get<{ Params: { id: string } }>(
    '/:id',
    { schema: getUserSchema },
    async (req) => service.findById(req.params.id)
  )

  fastify.post(
    '/',
    { schema: createUserSchema },
    async (req) => service.create(req.body)
  )
}

export default usersPlugin

bytes.topic: Middleware — request pipeline, global vs route-specific, ordering matters
bytes.title: Fastify Middleware — Hook Lifecycle, Scope, and Why Order Is Everything
bytes.body: Fastify has no Express-style `app.use()`. Middleware is hooks — and hooks have a strict lifecycle: `onRequest → preParsing → preValidation → preHandler → handler → onSend → onResponse`. Register hooks globally with `fastify.addHook()` or scope them per-route via the route options object. Scoping is done through encapsulation: wrap routes in `fastify.register()` and any hooks added inside that scope are invisible outside it. Order within a scope is registration order — a `preHandler` auth check registered after a logging hook runs second, not first. This trips up teams who assume plugin order doesn't matter. A common pattern: register global `onRequest` rate-limiting, then per-plugin `preHandler` auth, then per-route `preValidation` for input schema coercion. One subtle gotcha — `fastify.addHook('onRequest', ...)` called inside a plugin without `fastify-plugin` wrapping is automatically scoped. Wrap with `fp()` to promote it to global scope. Miss this and your auth hook silently skips unregistered routes.

bytes.topic: Testing — unit vs integration, mocking HTTP, test database strategy
bytes.title: Fastify Testing — Unit vs Integration, Mocking HTTP, and Test Database Strategy
bytes.body:
Fastify's built-in `inject()` method eliminates the need for a real HTTP server in integration tests — call `app.inject({ method: 'GET', url: '/users/1' })` and get a full response object including status, headers, and payload. This makes integration tests fast without sacrificing coverage of your route handlers, hooks, and serialization schemas.

For unit tests, isolate business logic by extracting it from route handlers and testing it directly. Avoid testing Fastify internals — test your own code.

For mocking downstream HTTP calls, use `@fastify/undici` or wrap `fetch`/`axios` behind a service interface, then swap it with a mock via Fastify's `decorate()` in test setup. Register the mock before calling `app.ready()`.

For database strategy, use a real PostgreSQL instance (Docker) with transaction rollbacks per test — wrap each test in `BEGIN`/`ROLLBACK` using your pool directly. Register the pool via `fastify.decorate('db', pool)` so the same interface works in tests and production. Never mock your ORM — schema mismatches will bite you in production.

bytes.topic: Error Handling — global error handlers, consistent error response format
bytes.title: Fastify Global Error Handling — Consistent Error Responses at Scale
bytes.body:
Fastify's `setErrorHandler` is your single choke point for all unhandled errors — register it once on the root instance and every thrown error, including those from plugins and child scopes, flows through it. Unlike Express middleware, Fastify passes a fully typed `FastifyError` with a `statusCode` property, so you can distinguish a 404 from a 500 without parsing message strings.

For validation errors, Fastify auto-generates a 400 with a `message` field from Ajv. Override the shape inside `setErrorHandler` by checking `error.validation` — it's a non-null array when the error originated from schema validation.

Always return a consistent envelope: `{ success, code, message, details? }`. This prevents clients from branching on wildly different error shapes. Pair this with `reply.log.error(error)` inside the handler so OpenTelemetry traces capture the stack without leaking it to the response. Avoid throwing raw `Error` objects — use `fastify.httpErrors` (from `@fastify/sensible`) to get pre-typed HTTP errors with correct status codes baked in.

bytes.topic: Authentication — JWT validation, session handling, auth middleware patterns
bytes.title: Fastify JWT Auth — Validation, Hooks, and Route-Level Guards
bytes.body:
Fastify's `@fastify/jwt` plugin wires JWT validation directly into the request lifecycle via decorators and hooks. After registering the plugin with your secret, `fastify.authenticate` becomes a reusable `preHandler` hook you attach per-route or at the scope level.

The plugin decorates `request` with `request.jwtVerify()`, which validates the token from the `Authorization: Bearer` header, verifies the signature, checks `exp`, and populates `request.user` with the decoded payload — all in one call. If verification fails, it throws a 401 automatically.

For role-based guards, compose a second `preHandler` after `authenticate` that reads `request.user.role`. Fastify's hook array runs sequentially, so ordering matters: verify first, authorize second.

Avoid session-based auth in Fastify unless you explicitly need server-side state — `@fastify/session` with `@fastify/cookie` works, but it fights Fastify's stateless, high-throughput design. JWTs with short expiry and a Redis-backed refresh token blocklist (checked in the `preHandler`) give you revocation without sessions.

Scope your `authenticate` hook at the plugin boundary using `fastify.register()` with `{ prefix }` to protect entire route groups without repeating the hook on every route.

bytes.topic: Input Validation — schema validation at the boundary, sanitization, error responses
bytes.title: Fastify Input Validation — Schema-First Boundaries with Ajv
bytes.body:
Fastify compiles your JSON Schema into optimized Ajv validators at startup, not per-request. Declare a schema on every route and Fastify rejects invalid payloads before your handler is ever called — zero manual validation code needed.

Attach schemas via `schema.body`, `schema.querystring`, `schema.params`, and `schema.headers`. Fastify uses `schema.response` to both validate and serialize outgoing data, stripping undeclared fields automatically — a built-in sanitization layer.

When validation fails, Fastify emits a 400 with a structured error from `setErrorHandler`. Override it to normalize shape across your API. Avoid catching validation errors inside handlers; let them bubble to the error handler so every boundary failure is handled uniformly.

Use `addSchema` + `$ref` to share schemas across routes without duplication. For string inputs that must match a pattern (emails, slugs), declare `format` or `pattern` directly in schema rather than sanitizing post-parse. Fastify's serialization also uses the response schema to fast-serialize JSON via `fast-json-stringify`, so tight schemas improve throughput as a side effect.

bytes.topic: Security — CORS, rate limiting, security headers, injection prevention
bytes.title: Fastify Security Hardening — CORS, Rate Limiting, Headers, and Injection Defense
bytes.body: Fastify's plugin ecosystem handles the full security surface out of the box. Register `@fastify/helmet` to set secure HTTP headers (CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options) in one call. For CORS, `@fastify/cors` lets you whitelist origins per-route or globally — avoid `origin: true` in production. Rate limiting via `@fastify/rate-limit` operates on the raw request level with Redis-backed storage for distributed deployments; set `max` and `timeWindow` per route using `config.rateLimit` in route options for fine-grained control. For injection prevention, never interpolate request params into raw SQL — use `@fastify/postgres` with parameterized queries (`$1, $2` placeholders). Fastify's schema-based validation via JSON Schema (the `schema.body`, `schema.querystring`, `schema.params` route options) is your first injection firewall: unknown properties are stripped by default because `additionalProperties` resolves to false when using `ajv-type-provider-typebox`. Always define strict schemas — it's not optional hygiene, it's load-bearing security.

bytes.topic: Serialization — request/response DTOs, camelCase vs snake_case, null handling
bytes.title: Fastify Serialization — Schema-Driven DTOs, Case Conventions, and Null Safety
bytes.body:
Fastify's serialization pipeline uses `fast-json-stringify` under the hood, bypassing `JSON.stringify` entirely. Define response schemas via `schema.response` and Fastify will serialize only declared fields — undeclared properties are silently stripped, which doubles as a data-leak guard.

For camelCase responses from a snake_case DB, add a serializer transform rather than mapping in every handler. Register a custom `serializerCompiler` or use `@fastify/camelcase-keys` to coerce keys at the plugin boundary. Keep your domain objects snake_case internally; let the serialization layer own the translation.

Null handling is schema-driven: declare `{ type: ['string', 'null'] }` (JSON Schema draft-07 union syntax) to allow a field to be null. Omitting `null` from the type array causes Fastify to coerce nulls to `0` or `''` silently — a sharp edge that bites in production. For optional fields that may be absent entirely, use `{ type: 'string', nullable: true }` with the `@fastify/swagger` compatible notation, or omit the field from the schema and accept the stripping behavior intentionally.

bytes.topic: API Design — REST conventions, versioning strategy, OpenAPI generation
bytes.title: Fastify API Versioning + OpenAPI in One Shot
bytes.body:
Fastify's plugin encapsulation makes versioning a first-class pattern — no middleware gymnastics required. Register each API version as an isolated plugin under a prefixed scope, then attach `@fastify/swagger` and `@fastify/swagger-ui` at the root level to auto-generate a unified OpenAPI spec from your route schemas.

Every route schema you define via `schema: { body, querystring, params, response }` is both a runtime validator (via `ajv`) and a live OpenAPI contributor. Fastify never separates these concerns — validation IS documentation.

Version your routes by prefix (`/v1`, `/v2`) using `fastify.register()` with the `prefix` option. Within each version plugin, define `operationId` in your route schema to avoid collision in the merged spec. Use `{ exposeRoute: true }` on swagger-ui so `/documentation` is auto-mounted.

For deprecation, add `deprecated: true` inside the route's `schema` block — it propagates directly into the OpenAPI output with zero extra config. This keeps your versioning strategy, validation, and API docs in perfect sync across versions.

bytes.topic: Performance — connection pooling, async handlers, response compression
bytes.title: Fastify Performance Trio: Connection Pooling, Async Handlers, and Response Compression
bytes.body:
Fastify's performance ceiling depends on three levers working together. For connection pooling, use `@fastify/postgres` or `@fastify/mysql` — both wrap `pg-pool` and `mysql2` and expose a decorated `fastify.pg` or `fastify.mysql` instance scoped to the server lifecycle, preventing connection leaks that kill throughput under load.

Async handlers are non-negotiable. Fastify's router natively awaits returned promises, so dropping `async/await` on route handlers lets the event loop stay unblocked during I/O. Never mix callbacks with `reply.send()` inside async handlers — Fastify will double-send and throw.

For compression, `@fastify/compress` hooks into Fastify's `onSend` lifecycle hook and negotiates `gzip`, `deflate`, or `br` via the `Accept-Encoding` header automatically. Set `threshold: 1024` to skip compressing small payloads where overhead exceeds savings. Combine with `encodings: ['br', 'gzip']` to prefer Brotli on supported clients. Together, these three plugins — registered once at startup — compound: pooled connections reduce latency, async handlers maximize concurrency, and compression cuts wire time.

bytes.topic: Configuration — env vars, settings classes, secrets management
bytes.title: Fastify Config — Env Vars, Typed Settings, and Secrets Done Right
bytes.body:
Fastify has no built-in config layer, but `@fastify/env` solves this cleanly. It validates environment variables against a JSON Schema at startup and decorates the Fastify instance with a typed `config` object — meaning your app crashes early on misconfiguration rather than at runtime when a secret is first accessed.

Register `@fastify/env` before any plugin that needs config, since Fastify's plugin encapsulation means decoration order matters. Use `dotenv` only locally; in production, inject vars through your container runtime or Azure App Settings — never commit `.env` files.

For secrets (DB passwords, API keys), avoid storing them in `fastify.config` long-term. Pull them at startup from Azure Key Vault or AWS Secrets Manager using the SDK, then decorate the instance manually with `fastify.decorate('secrets', ...)`. This keeps secrets out of your schema definition and prevents accidental serialization via `fastify.config` in logs or error responses.

Combining `@fastify/env` for typed app settings with a separate secrets decorator gives you validation, type safety, and secret isolation in one clean pattern.

bytes.topic: Logging — structured logs, correlation IDs, request logging middleware
bytes.title: Fastify Structured Logging — Correlation IDs and Request Tracing Built In
bytes.body:
Fastify ships with pino as its default logger, giving you structured JSON logs out of the box — no setup required. Every request gets its own child logger accessible via `request.log`, which automatically includes `reqId` for correlation. You can customize the ID strategy with `genReqId` on the server instance.

For distributed tracing, propagate incoming `x-request-id` headers into Fastify's `reqId` so logs chain across services. Register a `onRequest` hook to extract and forward the header, then decorate the request with it for use in handlers.

Pino's child loggers are zero-copy — `request.log.info({ userId })` merges context without cloning the base logger. Use `serializers` at the server level to redact sensitive fields like `authorization` headers globally.

Set `logger: { level: 'info', redact: ['req.headers.authorization'] }` in server options. In production, pipe pino output through `pino-pretty` only in dev — in prod, ship raw JSON to your collector (Loki, Datadog, etc.) for structured querying.

bytes.topic: Dev Tooling — hot reload, code generation, scaffolding CLI
bytes.title: Fastify Dev Tooling — Hot Reload, Code Generation, and Scaffolding CLI
bytes.body:
Fastify's official CLI (`fastify-cli`) is the fastest path from zero to running server. `fastify generate my-app` scaffolds a project with a pre-wired plugin structure, `app.js` entry point, and `plugins/` directory following Fastify's encapsulation model — not a blank Express clone.

For hot reload during development, pair `fastify-cli` with its built-in `--watch` flag. Running `fastify start --watch src/app.js` uses `chokidar` under the hood to restart only when source files change, preserving Fastify's plugin DAG integrity on each reload.

Code generation goes further with `fastify generate plugin auth` — it emits a properly scoped plugin file with `fp()` wrapping from `fastify-plugin`, ensuring decorators registered inside don't leak scope unless you intend them to.

For monorepos or custom scaffolding, `@fastify/restartable` lets you programmatically restart a Fastify instance without killing the process — useful for test harnesses or config-driven reload in staging environments. Combine it with `fastify.inject()` to verify routes survive restarts without spinning up a real HTTP server.

bytes.topic: Background Jobs — task queues, scheduled jobs, worker process patterns
bytes.title: Fastify Background Jobs — Queues, Schedules, and Worker Patterns
bytes.body:
Fastify has no built-in job queue, but its plugin system makes integration clean. The go-to pairing is `fastify-bullmq` (a thin wrapper over BullMQ + Redis). Register it as a plugin so the queue shares the Fastify lifecycle — `onClose` hooks drain queues gracefully on shutdown, preventing job loss during deploys.

For scheduled jobs, avoid `setInterval` in your server process. Instead, define a dedicated worker entrypoint that imports your queue definitions but never calls `fastify.listen()`. This separates HTTP concerns from CPU-bound processing and lets you scale workers independently.

Use `fastify.decorate('queue', queue)` to expose the BullMQ queue instance across routes, keeping enqueue calls inside route handlers rather than bleeding queue logic into business services. For job definitions, colocate the processor function with its route feature folder — not in a global `workers/` directory — to stay aligned with Fastify's vertical-slice plugin model.

Enable `removeOnComplete: { count: 100 }` and `removeOnFail: { age: 86400 }` on every job to prevent Redis memory bloat in production.

bytes.topic: Framework-specific — Django batteries-included, FastAPI auto-docs, NestJS decorators, Phoenix LiveView
bytes.title: Fastify's Plugin System — The Batteries-Included Architecture You Build Yourself
bytes.body:
Fastify doesn't ship with an ORM, auth layer, or mailer baked in — but its encapsulated plugin system lets you compose exactly those batteries with surgical precision. Every `fastify.register()` call creates an isolated scope: decorators, hooks, and route prefixes defined inside don't leak out unless you explicitly use `fastify-plugin` to break encapsulation. This is Fastify's answer to Django's app registry — modular, but opt-in global state.

Practical pattern: register your database connector (e.g. `@fastify/postgres`) at root scope via `fastify-plugin`, then register your domain routes as child plugins. Child plugins inherit `fastify.pg` but remain isolated from each other's hooks. Combine this with `fastify.decorate()` for request-scoped context and `addHook('onRequest', ...)` per plugin for auth guards — you get Django-style cohesion without the magic. Auto-documentation comes free via `@fastify/swagger`, which reads your `schema` objects on each route definition and emits OpenAPI — no decorators, no reflection, just the JSON Schema you already wrote for validation.

bytes.topic: Monitoring — health check endpoints, metrics exposure, distributed tracing
bytes.title: Fastify Monitoring — Health Checks, Prometheus Metrics, and Distributed Tracing
bytes.body: Fastify's plugin ecosystem covers the full observability stack with minimal boilerplate. For health checks, `@fastify/under-pressure` automatically monitors event loop lag, heap usage, and RSS memory, exposing a `/health` endpoint that returns `503` when thresholds are breached — no manual polling required. Pair it with `fastify-metrics` to mount a `/metrics` endpoint that exports Prometheus-compatible data including default Node.js metrics and any custom counters you register via the bundled `prom-client` instance. For distributed tracing, `@opentelemetry/instrumentation-fastify` hooks into Fastify's request lifecycle via its `onRequest` and `onResponse` hooks under the hood, automatically propagating W3C `traceparent` headers and creating spans per route. You get trace context on `request.span` without manual instrumentation. The key insight: all three plugins attach to the same Fastify instance and share its lifecycle — register them before `fastify.listen()` and they inherit your existing route prefix and `onError` hooks. Use `fastify.addHook('onClose', ...)` to flush OTel spans on graceful shutdown.
